s14.title: Disclosure of interest
s14.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity has established a conflict-of-interest disclosure framework aligned with the Committee governance requirements, including standing declarations, meeting protocols, and a register of disclosed interests.

evidenceItems.name: Conflict of Interest Policy and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Board-approved policy requiring disclosure of direct or indirect interests, with procedures for recusal from affected deliberations
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy covers both direct and indirect interests
Clear recusal process documented
Applies to Committee members and relevant governance bodies
Annual review cycle maintained
commonGaps: No formal conflict-of-interest policy
Policy exists but does not reference Act 854 obligations
Recusal procedures not defined
No annual review or update process

evidenceItems.name: Conflict of Interest Register
evidenceItems.description: Register of all disclosed interests, including meeting dates, nature of interest, and recusal decisions
whatGoodLooksLike: All disclosures recorded with dates
Nature of interest described in sufficient detail
Recusal decisions documented
Register reviewed at each Committee meeting
commonGaps: No formal register maintained
Disclosures recorded in minutes but not in a searchable register
Register not kept up to date
Standing declarations not refreshed annually

evidenceItems.name: Committee Meeting Minutes (Interest Disclosures)
evidenceItems.description: Minutes of Committee meetings recording any interest disclosures made, in compliance with s14 recording requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Standing agenda item for interest disclosures at each meeting
Nil returns recorded when no interests to declare
Details of disclosed interests captured accurately
Minutes signed and approved
commonGaps: No standing agenda item for disclosures
Nil returns not recorded (impossible to verify compliance)
Disclosure details insufficient
Minutes not formally approved

auditTips: Review at least 4 consecutive Committee meeting minutes for evidence of standing disclosure agenda items
Cross-reference the conflict register against known business relationships of Committee members
Verify that nil returns are recorded when no interests are declared — absence of records is not compliance evidence

s15.title: Chief Executive may issue directives
s15.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity maintains a comprehensive register of all CE directives, has conducted compliance assessments for each applicable directive, and can demonstrate implementation progress or completion.

evidenceItems.name: CE Directive Register
evidenceItems.description: Register tracking all CE directives issued under the Act, including directive references, applicability assessment, compliance status, and implementation timelines
whatGoodLooksLike: All known CE directives listed with reference numbers
Applicability assessment documented for each directive
Compliance status tracked with RAG ratings
Implementation timelines and responsible owners assigned
commonGaps: No directive register maintained
Directives received but not tracked systematically
Applicability assessment not performed
No responsible owners assigned for implementation

evidenceItems.name: Directive Implementation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence packs demonstrating implementation of each applicable CE directive, including technical configurations, policy updates, and process changes
whatGoodLooksLike: Evidence mapped to each directive requirement
Implementation completed within specified timelines
Technical and procedural controls verified
Sign-off from compliance officer
commonGaps: Implementation not tracked per directive
Timelines missed without documented justification
Technical requirements not verified
No formal sign-off process

evidenceItems.name: Directive Monitoring and Update Process
evidenceItems.description: Documented process for monitoring new and amended CE directives, assessing applicability, and initiating compliance activities
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular monitoring of NACSA publications for new directives
Defined process for applicability assessment
Trigger mechanisms for compliance activities
Process tested and documented
commonGaps: No proactive monitoring of new directives
Reliance on informal notification rather than systematic monitoring
No defined process for responding to new directives

auditTips: Request the CE directive register and verify it is current — compare against publicly available directive list from NACSA
Check implementation timelines against directive deadlines to identify any breaches
Verify that the entity has a process for identifying new directives, not just responding to those already known

s16.title: Secretariat to the Committee
s16.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that NACSA secretariat functions are documented and operational, providing effective administrative support to the National Cyber Security Committee.

evidenceItems.name: Committee Administrative Framework
evidenceItems.description: Documented framework for secretariat functions including meeting scheduling, agenda preparation, minutes distribution, and action tracking
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular meeting schedule established and maintained
Agenda preparation process documented
Minutes distributed within defined timeframe
Action items tracked to completion
commonGaps: No formal secretariat procedures
Minutes delayed or not distributed
Action items not tracked
Meeting records incomplete

evidenceItems.name: Committee Meeting Records Archive
evidenceItems.description: Complete archive of all Committee meetings including agendas, papers, minutes, attendance records, and action tracking logs
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete meeting record set for all sessions
Attendance records maintained
Decision records clearly captured
Archive securely stored and accessible
commonGaps: Incomplete meeting records
Attendance not recorded
Decisions not clearly documented
Archive not secured or backed up

auditTips: Review the completeness of meeting records — every scheduled meeting should have agendas, minutes, and attendance
Check action tracking to verify Committee decisions are followed through to implementation
Verify that meeting records are stored securely given the sensitive nature of NCII discussions

s17.title: Designation of NCII entity and NCII sector lead
s17.auditorFocus: NACSA assessors will verify that the entity has been formally designated by written notice, understands the scope of its NCII designation, and has initiated compliance programs for all Part IV obligations. They will check for the designation notice, board briefing records, and evidence that the 30-day appeal window was considered.

evidenceItems.name: NCII Designation Notice
evidenceItems.description: Original written notice from the CE designating the entity as an NCII entity, including the scope of designated infrastructure
whatGoodLooksLike: Original CE designation letter on file
Clear identification of designated NCII assets in scope
Date of designation and effective date documented
commonGaps: Designation notice not retained or misfiled
Scope of designated NCII not clearly understood by operational teams
No communication of designation to relevant business units

evidenceItems.name: NCII Asset Register
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive inventory of all assets designated as NCII, including system names, descriptions, criticality classifications, owners, and interdependencies
whatGoodLooksLike: Complete inventory of all designated NCII assets
Criticality classification for each asset
Owner assignment and review dates
Dependency mapping between NCII assets
commonGaps: Asset register is incomplete or outdated
No criticality classification
Shadow IT or cloud assets not captured
Dependencies not mapped

evidenceItems.name: Board Designation Briefing Record
evidenceItems.description: Minutes or record of board briefing on NCII designation, compliance obligations, penalty exposure, and director liability under s52
whatGoodLooksLike: Board minutes recording NCII designation discussion
Clear acknowledgement of compliance obligations
Decision to allocate resources for compliance program
Director liability awareness documented
commonGaps: No board-level awareness of NCII designation
Delegation without board oversight
No resource allocation decision recorded

auditTips: Confirm the designation notice is on file and dated — this establishes the compliance timeline
Check that the scope of designated NCII is clearly documented and understood by IT and compliance teams
Verify board-level awareness within 30 days of designation
If no appeal was filed, confirm the 30-day window was considered and a decision was documented

s18.title: Duties of NCII entity
s18.auditorFocus: Assessors will conduct a comprehensive review of the entity's compliance program across all s18(1)(a)-(g) duties. This is the broadest evidence requirement and will include review of cyber security measures, standards compliance, incident reporting capability, risk assessment and audit records, and information provision processes.

evidenceItems.name: NCII Cyber Security Policy
evidenceItems.description: Board-approved policy documenting the organization's commitment to NCII protection, covering all s18(1) duties
whatGoodLooksLike: Board-approved with clear effective date
Covers all s18(1)(a)-(g) duties explicitly
References applicable standards, CoPs and directives
Clear roles and responsibilities for NCII compliance
Annual review cycle documented
commonGaps: Generic IT security policy without NCII-specific provisions
Not board-approved or outdated
Missing reference to Act obligations
No designated accountability for NCII compliance

evidenceItems.name: NCII Compliance Program Charter
evidenceItems.description: Document establishing the compliance program, organizational structure, roles and reporting lines for NCII compliance management
whatGoodLooksLike: Named NCII compliance officer with clear mandate
Reporting lines to CISO and Board
Cross-functional team with defined responsibilities
Budget allocation documented
Compliance calendar covering all periodic obligations
commonGaps: No dedicated compliance program or officer
Compliance responsibilities dispersed without coordination
No budget allocation
Missing compliance calendar

evidenceItems.name: Quarterly Board Compliance Report
evidenceItems.description: Regular report to the Board on NCII compliance status, covering risk assessment results, audit findings, incidents, and remediation progress
whatGoodLooksLike: Quarterly cadence maintained
Covers all s18 duty areas
Includes risk RAG status
Tracks remediation progress against previous findings
Records board discussions and decisions
commonGaps: No regular board reporting
Reports lack specificity on NCII compliance
No tracking of remediation progress
Board not engaged in discussion

auditTips: Request the NCII compliance program charter or equivalent — this frames the entire compliance effort
Review at least 4 quarters of board reporting to assess reporting cadence and quality
Cross-reference the compliance calendar against actual completion dates for periodic obligations
Verify that the compliance program covers all seven duties in s18(1)(a)-(g)

s19.title: Duties of NCII sector lead
s19.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the sector lead has established coordination mechanisms for all NCII entities within its sector, maintains a sector cyber security posture view, develops sector-specific codes of practice, and regularly reports to the CE.

evidenceItems.name: Sector Coordination Framework
evidenceItems.description: Documented framework for coordinating and monitoring the cyber security posture of all NCII within the designated sector, including communication channels and escalation procedures
whatGoodLooksLike: All NCII entities in sector identified and registered
Regular coordination meetings scheduled and conducted
Communication channels established for threat sharing
Escalation procedures to CE documented
commonGaps: No formal coordination framework
Incomplete coverage of NCII entities in sector
Ad-hoc coordination without scheduled cadence
No escalation procedures to CE

evidenceItems.name: Sector Cyber Security Status Reports
evidenceItems.description: Regular reports to the CE on the state of cyber security across the sector, including threat landscape, compliance status, and incident trends
whatGoodLooksLike: Periodic reports submitted to CE as required
Coverage of all NCII entities in sector
Threat landscape assessment included
Compliance status and incident trends analysed
commonGaps: Reports not submitted or overdue
Incomplete sector coverage
No trend analysis or threat landscape context
Reports lack actionable insights

evidenceItems.name: Sector Code of Practice Development Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of sector-specific code of practice development, stakeholder consultation, and submission to CE for approval under s22
whatGoodLooksLike: CoP drafts with version history
Stakeholder consultation records
Submission to CE for approval documented
Alignment with Act requirements verified
commonGaps: No sector-specific CoP developed
CoP development without NCII entity consultation
Draft not aligned with Act requirements
Submission to CE delayed or incomplete

auditTips: Verify that the sector lead has identified all NCII entities within its sector — completeness is critical
Check the cadence and quality of sector reports to the CE
Ask for evidence of stakeholder consultation in CoP development to ensure sector-specific relevance

s20.title: Provision of information
s20.auditorFocus: Assessors will evaluate the entity's ability to respond to CE information requests within specified timeframes, including the completeness and accuracy of information, and the internal processes that enable rapid response.

evidenceItems.name: NCII Information Response Procedure
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure for receiving, processing, and responding to CE information requests within the specified timeframe
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear workflow from CE request receipt to response submission
Named responsible officers and escalation paths
Template response formats prepared
Timeframe tracking mechanism in place
commonGaps: No formal response procedure
Reliance on ad-hoc coordination to gather information
No timeframe tracking
Response authority not delegated to available personnel

evidenceItems.name: CE Information Request and Response Log
evidenceItems.description: Register tracking all CE information requests received, response dates, completeness status, and any follow-up communications
whatGoodLooksLike: All requests logged with dates and reference numbers
Response timestamps demonstrating timeframe compliance
Completeness of response documented
Follow-up requests tracked and resolved
commonGaps: No tracking of requests and responses
Response timeliness not documented
Incomplete responses not identified or remediated
No acknowledgement of receipt retained

evidenceItems.name: NCII Asset and Configuration Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Up-to-date documentation of NCII assets, system configurations, network diagrams, and data flows that enables rapid response to CE requests
whatGoodLooksLike: Asset register current and comprehensive
Network diagrams up to date
System configurations documented
Data flows mapped for all NCII
commonGaps: Asset register outdated or incomplete
No current network diagrams
Configuration documentation not maintained
Data flow mapping missing

auditTips: Request evidence of past CE information requests and verify response timeliness
Test the entity's readiness by requesting a sample information package — can they produce NCII documentation quickly?
Verify that NCII asset and configuration documentation is current enough to support rapid response

s21.title: Cyber security risk assessment
s21.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that risk assessments are conducted at prescribed intervals by CE-approved assessors, using appropriate methodology, and that results are submitted to both the CE and sector lead within prescribed timeframes. They will also review the risk treatment plan and its implementation progress.

evidenceItems.name: Risk Assessment Report
evidenceItems.description: Complete cyber security risk assessment report for NCII, conducted by a CE-approved assessor
whatGoodLooksLike: Conducted within prescribed frequency
Performed by CE-approved person (verified licence)
Covers all designated NCII assets
Follows prescribed methodology
Clearly identifies threats, vulnerabilities, impacts and risk levels
Includes risk treatment recommendations
commonGaps: Assessment overdue or not conducted
Assessor not CE-approved
Scope does not cover all designated NCII
Methodology does not meet regulatory requirements
Risk ratings not justified

evidenceItems.name: CE Submission Acknowledgement
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of timely submission of risk assessment results to the CE and sector lead
whatGoodLooksLike: Submission within prescribed timeframe
Acknowledgement from CE/NACSA
Acknowledgement from sector lead
Complete assessment results submitted
commonGaps: Late submission
Submission to CE only (not sector lead)
No acknowledgement retained
Incomplete results submitted

evidenceItems.name: Risk Treatment Plan
evidenceItems.description: Plan documenting how identified risks will be addressed, with actions, owners, timelines and resource requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: Treatment action for every high/critical risk
Named owners for each action
Realistic timelines
Board-approved resource allocation
Progress tracking mechanism
commonGaps: No treatment plan developed
Actions not assigned to owners
Timelines unrealistic or missed
No progress tracking
Board not informed of treatment requirements

evidenceItems.name: Approved Assessor Licence Verification
evidenceItems.description: Evidence verifying the risk assessor holds a valid CE-issued licence under Part V at the time of the assessment
whatGoodLooksLike: Copy of assessor's valid licence
Licence verification against CE register
Licence validity covers the assessment period
Assessor qualifications documented
commonGaps: No licence verification performed
Licence expired at time of assessment
Assessor not on CE register

auditTips: First check: is the most recent risk assessment within the prescribed period? Overdue assessments are the most common compliance gap
Verify the assessor's licence was valid at the time of the assessment by checking the CE register
Review submission timestamps against prescribed deadlines for both CE and sector lead submissions
Assess the risk treatment plan — findings without treatment plans suggest a tick-box approach rather than genuine risk management

s22.title: Codes of practice
s22.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity has identified all applicable codes of practice for its sector, conducted a gap analysis, and achieved compliance. They will check for evidence of ongoing CoP monitoring and implementation of any CoP updates.

evidenceItems.name: CoP Compliance Gap Analysis
evidenceItems.description: Documented assessment of the organization's compliance with applicable sector-specific code of practice requirements
whatGoodLooksLike: All applicable CoP requirements identified
Current compliance status per requirement
Gap identification with remediation actions
Compliance percentage tracked over time
commonGaps: No gap analysis conducted
CoP requirements not fully identified
Gap analysis outdated
No remediation plan for identified gaps

evidenceItems.name: CoP Implementation Evidence Pack
evidenceItems.description: Collection of evidence demonstrating compliance with each applicable CoP requirement (policies, procedures, configurations, test results)
whatGoodLooksLike: Evidence mapped to each CoP requirement
Evidence is current and dated
Includes both documentary and technical evidence
Reviewed and quality-checked
commonGaps: Evidence collection ad-hoc rather than systematic
Evidence outdated
Technical controls not verified
Evidence incomplete for some requirements

auditTips: Confirm which CoP version is applicable and whether the entity is aware of any pending updates
Look for a structured compliance register mapping each CoP requirement to evidence, rather than ad-hoc evidence collection
Verify that technical requirements in the CoP have been implemented, not just documented

s23.title: Cyber security audit
s23.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that audits are conducted at prescribed intervals by CE-approved auditors, covering the full regulatory compliance scope, and that results are submitted to both the CE and sector lead within prescribed timeframes. They will review audit findings and the remediation tracking process.

evidenceItems.name: Cyber Security Audit Report
evidenceItems.description: Complete audit report covering compliance with the Act, regulations, directives, codes of practice, and standards
whatGoodLooksLike: Conducted within prescribed frequency
Performed by CE-approved auditor (verified licence)
Covers full regulatory compliance scope
Includes technical testing results
Clear findings with severity ratings
Recommendations are specific and actionable
commonGaps: Audit overdue or not conducted
Auditor not CE-approved
Scope too narrow (missing regulations/directives)
No technical testing component
Findings vague or generic

evidenceItems.name: Audit Submission Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of timely submission of audit results to the CE and sector lead
whatGoodLooksLike: Submission within prescribed timeframe
Acknowledgement from CE/NACSA and sector lead
Complete audit results submitted
commonGaps: Late submission
Submission to one party only
No submission evidence retained

evidenceItems.name: Audit Remediation Tracker
evidenceItems.description: Tracking register for all audit findings with remediation actions, owners, timelines, and closure status
whatGoodLooksLike: All findings tracked with unique IDs
Named owners and realistic timelines
Regular progress reviews documented
Closed items verified
Escalation for overdue items
commonGaps: No systematic tracking of findings
Overdue items without escalation
No verification of finding closure
Findings carried forward across multiple audit cycles

auditTips: Check audit frequency compliance — the most recent audit must be within the prescribed period
Verify auditor licence validity at the time of the audit
Review the remediation tracker for carried-forward findings from prior audit cycles — these indicate systemic compliance issues
Confirm audit scope covers regulations, directives, and CoPs — not just the Act itself

s24.title: Standards of cyber security
s24.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity has identified all applicable CE-determined standards, conducted gap analyses, and achieved compliance within specified implementation periods.

evidenceItems.name: CE Standards Register
evidenceItems.description: Register of all CE-determined cyber security standards applicable to the entity, with compliance status tracking
whatGoodLooksLike: All applicable CE standards identified and listed
Compliance status per standard tracked
Implementation timelines documented
Responsible owners assigned
commonGaps: Incomplete identification of applicable standards
No compliance tracking mechanism
Standards register not updated when new standards issued
No ownership assignment

evidenceItems.name: Standards Gap Analysis Reports
evidenceItems.description: Gap analysis reports comparing current security posture against each applicable CE-determined standard, with remediation plans
whatGoodLooksLike: Gap analysis conducted for each applicable standard
Current compliance level quantified
Remediation actions identified with timelines
Progress tracked to completion
commonGaps: No gap analysis conducted
Gap analysis does not cover all standard requirements
No remediation plan developed
Remediation actions stalled without escalation

evidenceItems.name: Standards Implementation Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Technical and procedural evidence demonstrating compliance with each applicable CE standard requirement
whatGoodLooksLike: Evidence mapped to each standard requirement
Both technical and procedural controls demonstrated
Evidence current and dated
Independent verification where applicable
commonGaps: Evidence collection ad-hoc
Technical controls not independently verified
Evidence outdated
Gaps in coverage across standard requirements

auditTips: Cross-reference the entity's standards register against the full list of CE-determined standards to check completeness
Verify that gap analyses are recent and track remediation progress to completion
Pay particular attention to technical evidence — policy compliance alone is not sufficient for CE standards

s25.title: Duty to provide information on threats
s25.auditorFocus: Assessors will evaluate the entity's threat intelligence sharing capability, including the processes, channels, and timeliness of sharing threat information with the CE that may affect other NCII.

evidenceItems.name: Threat Information Sharing Procedure
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure for identifying, assessing, and sharing threat intelligence with the CE, including criteria for what constitutes reportable threat information
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear criteria for reportable threats and incidents
Defined sharing channels and contacts at NACSA
Process for rapid assessment and escalation
Procedure tested through exercises
commonGaps: No formal threat sharing procedure
Criteria for reportable threats undefined or too narrow
No established communication channel with NACSA
Procedure not tested

evidenceItems.name: Threat Intelligence Sharing Log
evidenceItems.description: Log of all threat information shared with the CE, including dates, threat descriptions, assessment rationale, and acknowledgements
whatGoodLooksLike: All shared threat intelligence logged with timestamps
Assessment rationale documented for each share
Acknowledgement from CE/NACSA recorded
Log includes both shared and assessed-as-not-reportable items
commonGaps: No log of shared intelligence maintained
Only incidents shared, not threats
No record of assessment decisions for threats not shared
Timeliness not tracked

evidenceItems.name: Threat Detection and Assessment Capability Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of threat detection tools, feeds, and processes that enable identification of threats relevant to other NCII
whatGoodLooksLike: Threat intelligence feeds operational and monitored
SOC has capability to assess cross-entity threat relevance
Automated detection rules for known threat patterns
Regular threat landscape reviews conducted
commonGaps: Limited threat detection capability
No assessment of whether threats affect other NCII
SOC focused only on internal threats
No threat intelligence feeds subscribed

auditTips: Ask the SOC whether they have ever identified a threat that could affect other NCII entities — if yes, verify it was shared with CE
Check that the threat sharing criteria are broad enough to capture relevant threats, not just confirmed incidents
Verify the communication channel with NACSA is tested and operational

s26.title: Notification of cyber security incident
s26.auditorFocus: Assessors will evaluate the entity's incident detection capability, notification process, and compliance history. They will verify whether past incidents were notified within the 6-hour window, review the incident response plan and notification templates, and test response readiness through process walkthroughs.

evidenceItems.name: Incident Response Plan (IRP)
evidenceItems.description: Documented plan for detecting, classifying, responding to, and notifying cyber security incidents, with clear 6-hour notification workflow
whatGoodLooksLike: Board-approved IRP with clear effective date
6-hour notification workflow mapped with decision points
Roles and responsibilities defined including 24/7 contacts
Incident classification criteria aligned with 'significant impact' definition
Pre-drafted notification templates
Escalation procedures to CE and sector lead
commonGaps: No IRP or generic IT incident plan without s26 alignment
6-hour notification workflow not explicit
No 24/7 contact arrangements
Classification criteria undefined
No pre-drafted templates

evidenceItems.name: Incident Notification Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of all incident notifications submitted to the CE, including timestamps demonstrating 6-hour compliance
whatGoodLooksLike: Notification submitted within 6 hours of awareness for significant incidents
All prescribed information fields completed
Acknowledgement from NACSA received
Follow-up notifications provided as required
Timeline reconstruction showing awareness → classification → notification
commonGaps: Notifications late or not submitted
Incomplete prescribed information
No acknowledgement retained
No timeline documentation

evidenceItems.name: IRP Testing Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of IRP testing including tabletop exercises, simulations, and drills that specifically test the 6-hour notification workflow
whatGoodLooksLike: Semi-annual testing at minimum
Scenarios include 6-hour notification trigger
Lessons learned documented
Improvements implemented post-exercise
Key personnel participation recorded
commonGaps: No IRP testing conducted
Testing does not cover notification workflow
No lessons learned or improvements
Key decision-makers not involved in exercises

evidenceItems.name: 24/7 On-Call Roster
evidenceItems.description: Current roster of personnel authorized to make incident notification decisions and submit notifications to the CE outside business hours
whatGoodLooksLike: Named individuals with contact details
Delegated authority documented
Coverage for all time zones and leave periods
Roster regularly updated
commonGaps: No out-of-hours notification capability
Single point of failure
Roster outdated
No delegated authority for notification

evidenceItems.name: Post-Incident Review Reports
evidenceItems.description: Root cause analysis and lessons learned reports for all notified incidents
whatGoodLooksLike: Completed within 30 days of incident closure
Root cause analysis with evidence
Lessons learned with specific improvement actions
Improvements tracked to completion
Shared with relevant stakeholders
commonGaps: No post-incident reviews conducted
Reviews superficial without root cause analysis
Improvement actions not tracked
Findings not communicated to management

auditTips: Request a walkthrough of the 6-hour notification workflow with the incident response team — time the process
If any incidents occurred since designation, verify notification timelines by comparing SOC awareness timestamps against notification submission timestamps
Check whether IRP tabletop exercises included realistic scenarios that trigger s26 notification
Verify 24/7 coverage — call the on-call number during the assessment to verify response

s27.title: Chief Executive may share information
s27.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity understands that information provided to the CE under Part IV may be shared with other NCII entities, sector leads, or government agencies. They will check that the entity has classified information appropriately and understands information sharing boundaries.

evidenceItems.name: Information Sharing Classification Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that information submitted to the CE has been classified with appropriate sharing markings, and that the entity understands CE information sharing authority
whatGoodLooksLike: Information submitted to CE classified per internal policy
Awareness that CE may share information under s27
Sensitive information flagged with appropriate caveats where permitted
Internal stakeholders informed of information sharing implications
commonGaps: No awareness that submitted information may be shared
No classification applied to CE submissions
Business-sensitive information submitted without internal review
No process to review CE submissions before sending

evidenceItems.name: CE Information Sharing Awareness Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that relevant staff understand the CE information sharing authority and its implications for information provided under Part IV
whatGoodLooksLike: Training materials cover s27 information sharing provisions
Staff involved in CE reporting are aware of sharing implications
Review process ensures submissions do not contain unnecessary sensitive detail
Legal counsel advises on information sharing risks
commonGaps: Staff unaware of CE sharing authority
No review process for CE submissions
Oversharing of sensitive operational detail in submissions
Legal counsel not consulted

auditTips: Ask the compliance team whether they are aware that information submitted to CE can be shared under s27
Review CE submissions for unnecessarily detailed operational information that could be sensitive if shared
Check whether information classification is applied before CE submission

s28.title: Licensing of cyber security service providers
s28.auditorFocus: For service providers: assessors will verify licence validity, conditions compliance, and professional standards. For NCII entities: assessors will verify that engaged service providers hold valid licences.

evidenceItems.name: CE-Issued Licence
evidenceItems.description: Valid licence granted by the CE to provide cyber security services
whatGoodLooksLike: Licence currently valid and not expired/suspended/revoked
Covers all services being provided
Conditions clearly understood and documented
Renewal tracked before 30-day pre-expiry deadline
commonGaps: Licence expired without renewal
Services provided outside licence scope
Conditions not monitored

evidenceItems.name: Third-Party Licence Verification (for NCII entities)
evidenceItems.description: Records verifying that all engaged cyber security service providers hold valid CE-issued licences
whatGoodLooksLike: Licence verified before engagement
Periodic re-verification during service period
Register check evidence retained
Contract clauses requiring licence maintenance
commonGaps: No pre-engagement verification
No ongoing monitoring of licence status
Reliance on provider self-declaration without verification

auditTips: For NCII entities: request a list of all cyber security service providers and cross-reference against the CE register
For service providers: verify the licence has not been suspended or revoked since issuance
Check whether the licence covers the specific services being provided (SOC monitoring, pentest, IR)

s29.title: Duration and renewal of licence
s29.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that licence validity is monitored, renewal applications are submitted at least 30 days before expiry, and the renewal process maintains continuity of service authorization.

evidenceItems.name: Licence Validity and Renewal Tracker
evidenceItems.description: Register tracking licence validity period, expiry dates, renewal deadlines, and renewal application status
whatGoodLooksLike: Licence expiry date prominently tracked
30-day pre-expiry renewal deadline flagged
Renewal application timeline planned and documented
Automatic reminder system in place
commonGaps: No expiry tracking mechanism
30-day renewal deadline missed
Renewal treated as last-minute activity
No calendar reminders set

evidenceItems.name: Licence Renewal Application Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of renewal applications submitted, including application date, supporting documents, CE acknowledgement, and renewal outcome
whatGoodLooksLike: Application submitted at least 30 days before expiry
All required supporting documents included
CE acknowledgement of receipt retained
Renewal outcome and new validity period documented
commonGaps: Application submitted late (less than 30 days before expiry)
Incomplete supporting documents
No acknowledgement of receipt retained
Previous licence conditions not reviewed during renewal

auditTips: Check the date gap between renewal application submission and licence expiry — must be at least 30 days
Verify that the entity has a proactive renewal calendar rather than waiting for CE reminders
For NCII entities engaging providers: verify that provider licence renewals are monitored as part of vendor management

s30.title: Suspension and revocation of licence
s30.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity understands the grounds for licence suspension and revocation, has controls to prevent triggering events, and monitors compliance with licence conditions continuously.

evidenceItems.name: Licence Conditions Compliance Monitoring
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of ongoing monitoring of compliance with all licence conditions, with corrective actions taken for any deviations
whatGoodLooksLike: All licence conditions documented and understood
Regular self-assessment against conditions conducted
Deviations identified and corrected promptly
Compliance monitoring reports maintained
commonGaps: Licence conditions not reviewed after initial issuance
No self-assessment process
Deviations discovered only during external review
No corrective action tracking

evidenceItems.name: Licence Risk and Escalation Register
evidenceItems.description: Register tracking risks that could lead to licence suspension or revocation, including non-compliance events, regulatory warnings, and remediation status
whatGoodLooksLike: All potential licence risks identified
Early warning indicators monitored
Regulatory communications tracked
Remediation actions assigned and tracked
commonGaps: No licence risk register maintained
No early warning mechanisms
Regulatory warnings not escalated to management
Remediation delayed or not tracked

auditTips: Check whether the entity has received any CE warnings or show-cause notices — these are precursors to suspension/revocation
Verify that licence conditions are actively monitored, not just acknowledged at issuance
For NCII entities: verify that provider licence suspensions or revocations are detected through vendor monitoring

s32.title: Obligations of licensee
s32.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that licensed cyber security service providers maintain professional standards, protect client confidentiality, keep comprehensive service records, and comply with all licence conditions.

evidenceItems.name: Professional Standards Framework
evidenceItems.description: Documented framework covering competence, integrity, and professionalism standards for all cyber security service delivery staff
whatGoodLooksLike: Staff competency requirements defined per service type
Professional certification requirements documented
Code of ethics/conduct in place
Regular competency assessments conducted
commonGaps: No formal competency framework
No certification requirements
Code of conduct generic without cyber security specifics
No ongoing competency verification

evidenceItems.name: Client Information Protection Controls
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of information security controls protecting client confidentiality, including encryption, access controls, and secure data handling
whatGoodLooksLike: Client data classified and handled per classification policy
Encryption in transit and at rest for client data
Access restricted to authorized engagement team members
Secure disposal of client data post-engagement
commonGaps: No specific controls for client data protection
Broad access to client engagement data
No secure disposal process
Client data on personal devices without controls

evidenceItems.name: Service Delivery Records
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive records of all cyber security services provided, including engagement scope, findings, recommendations, and client communications
whatGoodLooksLike: Engagement records maintained for all services
Scope, methodology, findings clearly documented
Client communications and approvals recorded
Records indexed and retrievable
commonGaps: Incomplete engagement records
Findings not formally documented
Client communications not retained
Records scattered across systems without index

auditTips: Request a sample engagement file to assess the quality and completeness of service records
Verify that staff certifications are current and relevant to the services being delivered
Check client data handling controls including post-engagement data disposal procedures

s33.title: Effect of suspension or revocation
s33.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that service providers have contingency plans for licence suspension or revocation scenarios, and that NCII entities have contingency arrangements for provider licence disruptions.

evidenceItems.name: Service Continuity and Cessation Plan
evidenceItems.description: Plan addressing service cessation obligations upon licence suspension or revocation, including client notification, data handover, and outstanding liability management
whatGoodLooksLike: Cessation procedures documented for each service type
Client notification process defined
Data return and secure disposal procedures in place
Outstanding liabilities and obligations identified
commonGaps: No cessation plan developed
Client notification procedures undefined
No data handover process
Outstanding liabilities not assessed

evidenceItems.name: NCII Entity Vendor Contingency Arrangements
evidenceItems.description: For NCII entities: contingency arrangements for continuity of cyber security services if a provider licence is suspended or revoked
whatGoodLooksLike: Alternative provider identified for critical services
Contractual provisions for provider licence loss
Knowledge transfer arrangements in place
Service level continuity plan documented
commonGaps: No contingency for provider licence disruption
Single-vendor dependency without backup
No contractual protections for licence loss
Knowledge concentrated with single provider

auditTips: For providers: ask what happens to active client engagements if the licence is suspended tomorrow
For NCII entities: check whether vendor contracts address provider licence suspension or revocation scenarios
Verify that data return and disposal processes are documented and tested

s34.title: Power of investigation
s34.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity has procedures to cooperate with authorized officers exercising investigation powers, including readiness to provide access to premises, systems, documents, and personnel.

evidenceItems.name: Investigation Cooperation Protocol
evidenceItems.description: Documented protocol for cooperating with authorized officer investigations, including access to premises, systems, and documents
whatGoodLooksLike: Protocol covers premises access, system access, and document production
Named liaison officers designated
Legal counsel notification trigger defined
Staff briefed on cooperation obligations and rights
commonGaps: No investigation cooperation protocol
No liaison officer designated
Staff untrained on investigation scenarios
Legal counsel involvement not planned

evidenceItems.name: Evidence Preservation and Chain of Custody Procedure
evidenceItems.description: Procedure for preserving digital evidence and maintaining chain of custody when cooperating with investigations
whatGoodLooksLike: Evidence preservation procedures documented
Chain of custody forms and processes defined
Forensic readiness measures in place
Log integrity and tamper-evidence mechanisms operational
commonGaps: No evidence preservation procedures
Logs not protected against tampering
No forensic readiness capability
Chain of custody not understood

auditTips: Ask the CISO what would happen if an authorized officer arrived with an investigation warrant — walk through the response
Verify that log retention and integrity controls support forensic investigation requirements
Check whether evidence preservation procedures are included in incident response training

s37.title: Access to computerized data
s37.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity can provide authorized officers with access to computerized data, including passwords, encryption keys, and decryption codes for all NCII systems.

evidenceItems.name: Computerized Data Access Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Procedures for providing authorized officers with access to computerized data during investigations, including password provision, encryption key disclosure, and system access facilitation
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures cover all NCII system types
Emergency access mechanisms documented
Encryption key recovery or escrow in place
Process does not depend on single individuals
commonGaps: No procedures for regulatory system access
Encryption keys not recoverable
Single-person dependency for system access
Cloud-hosted systems without rapid access provision capability

evidenceItems.name: System Access and Credential Inventory
evidenceItems.description: Inventory of administrative credentials and access methods for all NCII systems, stored securely but retrievable for authorized officer requests
whatGoodLooksLike: All NCII systems have documented access methods
Credentials stored in enterprise password vault
Break-glass procedures documented and tested
Inventory includes cloud and third-party hosted systems
commonGaps: No centralized credential inventory
Some systems accessible only by departed staff
No break-glass procedures
Cloud system credentials not included

auditTips: Test: can the entity provide administrative access to each NCII system within a reasonable timeframe?
Verify that encryption key escrow or recovery mechanisms are functional, not just documented
Check for single-person dependencies in system access — what if the admin is unavailable?

s44.title: Obstruction
s44.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity has procedures in place to cooperate with authorized officers, including access protocols, document production processes, and staff awareness of cooperation obligations.

evidenceItems.name: Regulatory Cooperation Procedure
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure for receiving and cooperating with authorized officer investigations, inspections, and enforcement activities under Part VI
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure covers access facilitation, document production, and interview arrangements
Named liaison officers for regulatory interactions
Escalation path to legal counsel defined
Staff awareness of cooperation obligations
commonGaps: No formal cooperation procedure
Staff unaware of obligations to cooperate
No liaison officer designated
Legal counsel involvement not planned

evidenceItems.name: Access and Credential Provision Readiness
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the entity can provide passwords, encryption keys, decryption codes, and system access as required by authorized officers under the Act
whatGoodLooksLike: Key management procedures include regulatory access provisions
Emergency access procedures documented
Encryption key escrow or recovery mechanisms in place
Process tested through exercises
commonGaps: No regulatory access provisions in key management
Encryption keys not recoverable
No emergency access procedures
Process not tested

auditTips: Ask staff what they would do if an authorized officer arrived to conduct an investigation — test awareness
Verify that encryption key recovery procedures are functional, not just documented
Check whether the regulatory cooperation procedure has been communicated to reception and security staff

s48.title: Appeal to the Minister
s48.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity understands the appeal mechanism, has processes to evaluate CE decisions for potential appeal, and can file appeals within the 30-day window when appropriate.

evidenceItems.name: Appeal Rights Awareness and Decision Framework
evidenceItems.description: Documentation showing awareness of appeal rights under s48, including the 30-day filing window, and a framework for evaluating whether to appeal CE decisions
whatGoodLooksLike: Appeal rights documented in compliance handbook
30-day window tracked for each CE decision received
Decision framework for evaluating appeal merit
Legal counsel involvement defined
commonGaps: No awareness of appeal rights
30-day window expires without consideration
No framework for appeal decision-making
Legal counsel not consulted on CE decisions

evidenceItems.name: Appeal Filing Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of any appeals filed with the Minister, including the CE decision appealed, grounds for appeal, filing date, and appeal outcome
whatGoodLooksLike: Appeal filed within 30-day window
Grounds for appeal clearly articulated
Supporting evidence compiled
Minister decision recorded and actioned
commonGaps: Appeal filed late (outside 30-day window)
Insufficient grounds or evidence prepared
No tracking of appeal progress
Appeal outcome not communicated to affected teams

auditTips: Check whether any CE decisions were received and whether the 30-day appeal window was considered for each
Verify that appeal rights are covered in compliance training materials
If appeals were filed, review the quality of grounds and supporting evidence

s49.title: Obligation of secrecy
s49.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity has implemented controls to protect Act-related information, including access restrictions, classification procedures, and staff awareness of secrecy obligations.

evidenceItems.name: Information Classification and Handling Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Procedures for classifying and handling Act-related information (incident reports, risk assessments, audit results, threat intelligence)
whatGoodLooksLike: Classification scheme includes Act-related information category
Handling requirements defined (storage, transmission, disposal)
Access restricted to authorized personnel
Regular review of access rights
commonGaps: No specific classification for Act-related information
Handling procedures not defined
Overly broad access to sensitive Act-related data

evidenceItems.name: Staff Awareness and NDA Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of staff awareness training on secrecy obligations and signed NDAs for personnel with access to Act-related information
whatGoodLooksLike: All relevant staff trained on s49 obligations
NDAs signed by personnel with access
Training records maintained
Annual refresher training
commonGaps: No awareness of s49 obligations
NDAs not updated to reference Act 854
Training not conducted for new joiners

auditTips: Check whether NDAs and employment contracts reference Act 854 secrecy obligations specifically
Verify access controls on Act-related information — who has access to incident reports and risk assessment results?
Ask staff questions about their understanding of what information is covered by s49

s52.title: Offences by body corporate
s52.auditorFocus: Assessors will evaluate whether the Board and officers can demonstrate the due diligence defence — that any offence was committed without their knowledge/consent/connivance and that they exercised all due diligence to prevent it.

evidenceItems.name: Board Cyber Security Oversight Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of board-level engagement with NCII compliance including meeting minutes, compliance reports reviewed, decisions made, and resources allocated
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular board agenda item for cyber security
Board receives and discusses NCII compliance reports
Decisions on resource allocation documented
Board-approved cyber security strategy and budget
Board training on Act obligations including personal liability
commonGaps: No board-level cyber security oversight
Compliance reports not presented to board
No documented resource allocation decisions
Board unaware of personal liability under s52

evidenceItems.name: Director Due Diligence Evidence Pack
evidenceItems.description: Compilation of evidence that each director/officer exercised due diligence in preventing Act offences
whatGoodLooksLike: Training completion records for directors on Act obligations
Evidence of questioning and challenging management on compliance
Approval of adequate resources for NCII compliance
Establishment or oversight of compliance committee
commonGaps: No specific due diligence evidence maintained
Directors not trained on Act obligations
Passive governance approach — receive reports but no engagement

auditTips: This is the personal liability provision — directors will be very interested in understanding what constitutes sufficient due diligence
Look for evidence of active governance (questioning, challenging, deciding) not just passive receipt of reports
Verify that the 10x fine multiplier for body corporates has been communicated to the board and factored into risk assessments

s53.title: Prosecution
s53.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity understands the prosecution process, maintains records supporting a compliance defence, and has legal counsel arrangements for potential prosecution scenarios.

evidenceItems.name: Compliance Defence Evidence Portfolio
evidenceItems.description: Compilation of evidence demonstrating consistent good-faith compliance efforts, maintained as a defence portfolio in case of prosecution
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive compliance evidence systematically organized
Evidence covers all applicable Act obligations
Regular updates to portfolio maintained
Legal counsel has reviewed evidence sufficiency
commonGaps: No proactive compilation of defence evidence
Evidence scattered across departments without index
Legal counsel not engaged on compliance evidence strategy
Evidence gaps for key obligations

evidenceItems.name: Legal Counsel and Prosecution Readiness
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of legal counsel arrangements and awareness of Public Prosecutor consent requirement for Act 854 prosecutions
whatGoodLooksLike: Legal counsel with Act 854 expertise identified or retained
Prosecution risk assessed and communicated to Board
Legal response protocol documented
Key staff aware of their rights if investigated
commonGaps: No legal counsel identified for Act 854 matters
Board unaware of prosecution risk exposure
No legal response protocol
Staff unaware of rights during investigation
evidenceItems.format: Legal retainer / Board papers / Protocol document

auditTips: Ask whether the entity has considered prosecution risk and included it in the corporate risk register
Verify that legal counsel arrangements cover Act 854 specifically, not just general corporate legal support
Check whether the Board has been briefed on the prosecution risk and the Public Prosecutor consent requirement

s54.title: Compounding of offences
s54.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity understands the compounding (settlement) mechanism as an alternative to prosecution, and has processes to evaluate compounding offers and respond within specified timeframes.

evidenceItems.name: Compounding Process Awareness and Response Protocol
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the entity understands the compounding mechanism under s54, including the 50% fine cap, payment timeframe, and decision-making process for responding to compounding offers
whatGoodLooksLike: Compounding provisions documented in compliance handbook
Decision-making authority for compounding offers defined
Legal counsel involvement required for compounding decisions
Board notification process for compounding offers
commonGaps: No awareness of compounding mechanism
No decision-making authority defined for compounding offers
Legal counsel not involved in compounding decisions
Board not informed of compounding offers

evidenceItems.name: Compounding Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of any compounding offers received and responses, including decision rationale, payment records, and compliance remediation undertaken
whatGoodLooksLike: Compounding offer and response documented
Payment within specified timeframe evidenced
Decision rationale recorded with legal advice
Remediation of underlying offence documented
commonGaps: No records of compounding interactions
Payment timeline not tracked
No remediation of underlying issue after compounding
Board not informed of outcome

auditTips: Check whether the entity has ever received a compounding offer — if yes, verify response timeliness and remediation
Verify that the compounding mechanism is covered in compliance awareness training
Ensure that compounding is understood as a settlement option, not as a substitute for compliance improvement

s57.title: Transitional provision for NCII entities
s57.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that existing NCII operators registered with the CE within the prescribed transitional period, and that interim compliance measures were implemented pending formal designation.

evidenceItems.name: Transitional Registration Records

whatGoodLooksLike: Registration submitted within prescribed period
Acknowledgement from CE received and retained
Scope of existing NCII operations declared
Contact details and responsible officers identified
commonGaps: Registration missed or late
Incomplete registration information
No acknowledgement retained
Scope of operations understated

evidenceItems.name: Interim Compliance Program
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of interim compliance measures implemented between commencement and formal NCII designation, demonstrating good-faith compliance efforts
whatGoodLooksLike: Gap analysis against Act requirements conducted
Priority compliance actions identified and initiated
Interim reporting to management established
Resources allocated for full compliance program
commonGaps: No interim compliance activity during transitional period
Waited for formal designation before starting compliance
No gap analysis conducted
No resource allocation decisions made
evidenceItems.format: Gap analysis / Project plan / Board papers

auditTips: Verify transitional registration was timely — late registration indicates compliance culture issues
Check whether interim compliance measures were genuine or token efforts
For entities designated after the transitional period: this section may still provide due diligence evidence for s52 defence

s5.title: Functions of the Agency
s5.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity understands NACSA's functions and has established communication channels with the Agency for cyber security matters, policy implementation, and threat coordination.

evidenceItems.name: NACSA Liaison and Communication Records

whatGoodLooksLike: Designated NACSA liaison officer appointed within the entity

Escalation path to NACSA defined in incident response procedures

Communication with NACSA is ad hoc with no records
Staff unaware of how to contact NACSA
No integration of NACSA guidance into internal processes

evidenceItems.name: NACSA Directive and Guidance Tracking Register
evidenceItems.description: Register tracking NACSA-issued strategies, standards, guidelines, codes of practice, and directives, with implementation status for each
whatGoodLooksLike: All NACSA publications tracked with receipt date
Implementation status recorded per directive
Gap analysis performed for each new issuance
Board or management briefed on new obligations
commonGaps: No systematic tracking of NACSA publications
Directives received but not assessed for applicability
No gap analysis between current state and directive requirements
Delayed implementation with no tracking

auditTips: Ask the CISO how the entity stays informed of new NACSA directives and guidelines
Verify that NACSA communication channels are documented in the incident response plan
Check if the entity has participated in any NACSA-coordinated exercises or briefings

s6.title: Appointment and powers of Chief Executive
s6.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity is aware of the Chief Executive's powers under Act 854, including delegation authorities, and has processes to respond to Chief Executive directives.

whatGoodLooksLike: Key CE powers documented in compliance framework

commonGaps: No awareness of CE's statutory powers
No process for receiving and acting on CE directives
Compliance team unfamiliar with delegation provisions
No legal review step for CE-issued orders

evidenceItems.name: Regulatory Response Protocol
evidenceItems.description: Protocol for receiving, assessing, and implementing directives or orders from the Chief Executive or delegated officers
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented protocol for handling CE communications
Triage process for urgency classification of directives
Assigned responsibility for directive implementation
Tracking mechanism for compliance deadlines
commonGaps: No formal protocol for regulatory directives
Directives handled reactively with no systematic process
No deadline tracking for directive compliance
Unclear ownership for directive implementation

auditTips: Ask the compliance team what would happen if a directive arrived from the CE today — walk through the response
Verify that CE powers are referenced in the entity's compliance framework documentation
Check whether the entity distinguishes between CE directives and delegated officer directives

s7.title: Appointment of officers and servants
s7.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity can identify and cooperate with authorized officers of NACSA exercising enforcement powers, and has procedures for verifying officer credentials.

evidenceItems.name: Enforcement Visit Log

Follow-up actions tracked to completion
commonGaps: No log of enforcement visits maintained

Follow-up actions not tracked
No distinction between different types of regulatory visits

auditTips: Ask reception and security staff what they would do if a NACSA officer arrived unannounced

Verify that legal counsel is notified when authorized officers exercise enforcement powers

Board not briefed on national governance structure

evidenceItems.name: Committee Decision Tracking Register

whatGoodLooksLike: Relevant Committee decisions tracked with dates
Impact assessment performed for each decision
Implementation actions assigned and tracked
Regular updates to board on Committee developments
commonGaps: No tracking of Committee decisions
No impact assessment of national-level decisions
Sector-specific implications not analysed
Board uninformed of Committee activities

auditTips: Ask the compliance team how Committee decisions are communicated to the entity

Verify that sector-relevant Committee decisions have been assessed for compliance impact

s9.title: Functions of the Committee
s9.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity understands the Committee's functions — particularly sector determination, emergency coordination, and cross-agency coordination — and has processes to respond to Committee-driven requirements.

evidenceItems.name: National Emergency Coordination Readiness

whatGoodLooksLike: Emergency contact details registered with sector lead

Communication protocols for national emergencies established
Staff trained on coordination procedures

Never participated in national exercises
No communication protocol for national incidents
Staff unaware of national coordination mechanisms

auditTips: Ask whether the entity has participated in any national or sector-level cyber security exercises
Verify that the entity's NCII sector designation is documented and understood at board level

s10.title: Delegation of functions

Verify that delegation provisions are referenced in the compliance framework

s11.title: Meetings of the Committee
s11.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity understands Committee meeting procedures and quorum requirements, relevant when Committee decisions affecting the entity's sector may be challenged.

evidenceItems.name: Committee Governance Awareness Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the entity's legal and compliance teams understand Committee meeting procedures, quorum requirements, and voting rules for the purpose of validating Committee decisions
whatGoodLooksLike: Committee governance procedures documented for reference

Verify that the entity understands the evidentiary weight of confirmed Committee minutes

s13.title: Procedure at meetings
s13.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify awareness that the Committee may regulate its own procedure, meaning procedural rules may evolve and entities must stay informed of changes to engagement protocols.

evidenceItems.name: Committee Procedural Updates Monitoring

s31.title: Register of licensees

evidenceItems.name: Licensee Register Verification Records

whatGoodLooksLike: Register verification performed before vendor onboarding

evidenceItems.name: Own Licence Registration Confirmation

Registered particulars outdated or inaccurate
No process to update register on changes
Clients unable to verify provider's registration

auditTips: Ask procurement whether they verify service provider licences against the Chief Executive's register

s35.title: Search and seizure with warrant
s35.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity has procedures to cooperate with warrant-based searches, including premises access protocols, data access procedures, and evidence preservation during search operations.

evidenceItems.name: Warrant-Based Search Cooperation Procedure

evidenceItems.name: Seized Item Documentation and Tracking

s36.title: Search and seizure without warrant

evidenceItems.name: Emergency Search Event Records

No legal review of search lawfulness
Records insufficient for subsequent legal challenge

auditTips: Ask whether the entity's search cooperation procedures distinguish between warrant and warrantless scenarios

s38.title: Power to intercept communications
s38.auditorFocus: Assessors will verify that the entity has procedures to cooperate with lawful interception orders, including providing technical access to communication systems and preserving communication logs.

evidenceItems.name: Communication System Access Capability Assessment

s39.title: List of things seized
